title: Always pass timeout to requests calls
content:
Use requests.get(url, timeout=10) or higher. Without an explicit timeout, the call can hang the worker indefinitely on a slow upstream. Default httplib timeout is None.

Why: in async tasks or long-running services, hung sockets accumulate until the worker is unresponsive. Single retry with backoff handles transient failures.

When: every outbound HTTP call in production code. Tests can use connect+read tuple (3, 10) to fail fast.

title: Use connection pooling for SQLAlchemy in async services
content:
Configure pool_size, max_overflow, and pool_pre_ping=True on the engine. AsyncSession reuses the underlying DBAPI connection from the pool. Without pool_pre_ping a stale connection (after database restart, network hiccup) returns a confusing OperationalError on first query.

Why: asyncio runs many short-lived coroutines that each want a DB session. Without a pool you exhaust the database's max_connections in seconds.

When: any FastAPI/Starlette app with SQLAlchemy 2.0+ and PostgreSQL.

title: Use FastAPI Depends for shared resources, not globals
content:
Define resources (DB session, settings, Redis client) as Depends() callables. Each request gets a fresh instance from the factory, and tests can override Depends() in app.dependency_overrides.

Why: globals + module-state break test isolation, leak across requests, and make graceful shutdown hard. Depends() is FastAPI's idiomatic dependency injection — type-checked by Pydantic, lifecycle-managed by Starlette.

When: any resource that needs per-request scope or test override.

title: Lifespan context manager over startup/shutdown events
content:
FastAPI 0.110+ deprecates app.on_event in favour of @asynccontextmanager lifespan(app). Yields the started state, awaits cleanup on shutdown.

Why: lifespan is structured concurrency aware (TaskGroup, AnyIO), exception-safe, and makes the start/stop pair impossible to mis-order. on_event handlers can silently fail in subtle async contexts.

When: any FastAPI app with persistent connections (DB pool, Redis, Kafka).

title: BackgroundTasks for fire-and-forget, not for important work
content:
FastAPI BackgroundTasks runs after the response is sent, in the same process. Suitable for analytics ping, audit log write. Not suitable for retryable work or anything you need to confirm completed.

Why: if the worker dies between response and background task completion, the work is lost. There's no retry, no dead-letter queue, no observability.

When: low-stakes side effects only. For real work, use Celery/RQ/Arq with a broker.

title: Pydantic model_validate over BaseModel(**data)
content:
model_validate() is the v2 recommended entry point. It runs validators and supports strict mode. **kwargs construction skips field validation entirely on plain assignment.

Why: silent data corruption is the worst kind. model_validate raises on bad input; **kwargs may swallow type errors via permissive coercion.

When: parsing untrusted input (request body, env vars, JSON files).

title: computed_field over @property for serialized derived values
content:
Pydantic v2 computed_field decorator includes the property in model_dump() and the OpenAPI schema. Plain @property does not — model_dump() ignores it and clients see no field.

Why: clients of an API contract need the derived field documented. Hidden properties cause schema drift and silent integration breaks.

When: any derived value that should appear in API responses or generated docs.

title: pytest fixtures with scope='session' for expensive setup
content:
Database engines, Docker containers, model loads — fixture scope='session' runs the setup once per pytest invocation. Default 'function' scope re-runs per test, blowing up suite time.

Why: 200 tests × 3s engine init = 10 minutes of pure overhead. Session scope drops it to 3s total.

When: anything heavier than a few ms to construct, where each test wants a fresh sub-resource (transaction, db row) but not a fresh root.

title: pytest-asyncio mode='auto' for async test bodies
content:
Set asyncio_mode = 'auto' in pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options]. Then async def test_X() runs without @pytest.mark.asyncio on every function.

Why: forgetting the mark silently SKIPS the test (no error). 'auto' mode catches the async def and runs it.

When: any pytest project with async test bodies.

title: Alembic autogenerate is a draft, never a finished migration
content:
Run 'alembic revision --autogenerate -m msg' then read the generated file end-to-end. Autogen misses: server_default changes, JSON column type swaps, named constraint renames, index option changes, custom check constraints.

Why: silent migration drift is how a shop ends up with prod-vs-staging schema mismatch. Treat autogen as a starting point, not a deliverable.

When: every alembic revision before commit.

title: ruff check + ruff format replace flake8 + black + isort
content:
Single Rust binary, ~100x faster, identical configuration via pyproject.toml. ruff check covers most pep8 + import sorting + many flake8 plugins; ruff format replaces black with same output style.

Why: faster CI = faster feedback. One tool to configure instead of three. Drop dependencies from dev environment.

When: any new Python project; migrating existing projects via 'ruff check --fix' for the bulk pass.

title: uv pip install over pip install for venv setup
content:
uv is 10-100x faster than pip and supports lockfiles natively. uv pip install -e '.[dev]' is a drop-in for the standard editable+extras install.

Why: faster dev onboarding, fewer transient dep resolution failures. Lockfile (uv.lock) gives reproducible installs that pip can't match without pip-tools.

When: new venvs, CI environment setup, Docker image builds.

title: Never use eval() or exec() on user-supplied input
content:
Trigger: needing to evaluate a math expression, JSON-like syntax, or 'simple' DSL provided by an external caller.

Consequence: arbitrary code execution. The attacker reads your environment variables, your DB credentials, your filesystem.

Alternative: ast.literal_eval for safe Python literals. json.loads for JSON. simpleeval library or a real parser for math.

title: Never store API keys in source code
content:
Trigger: 'just for now' hardcoded credential, or a comment with the key 'so I remember'.

Consequence: git history is forever. Even if you remove the key in the next commit, it's in the diff. GitHub scans new pushes and notifies the provider; rotation is mandatory and embarrassing.

Alternative: environment variables loaded via pydantic-settings or os.environ. .env files added to .gitignore. Secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Vault) for production.

title: Never use mutable default arguments
content:
Trigger: def f(items=[]): or def g(cfg={}): — Python evaluates the default ONCE at function definition. Subsequent calls share the same list/dict.

Consequence: state leaks across invocations. Test 1 mutates the default, Test 2 sees Test 1's state, debugging takes hours.

Alternative: def f(items=None): items = items if items is not None else []. Or use immutable defaults: tuples, frozensets.

title: Never write bare except: clauses
content:
Trigger: 'I just want to catch any error and log it.'

Consequence: catches KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, MemoryError, asyncio.CancelledError. The user can't Ctrl-C your script. asyncio task cancellation gets swallowed and tasks leak.

Alternative: except Exception as e: for general catch (still misses SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt by design). Be specific where possible: except (HTTPError, Timeout) as e:.

title: Never run CPU-bound code in the asyncio event loop
content:
Trigger: heavy parsing, regex on large input, hashing, image manipulation inside async def handler.

Consequence: blocks the event loop. Other concurrent requests stall; latency spikes; healthchecks time out.

Alternative: asyncio.to_thread(fn, *args) for I/O-bound-but-blocking. concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor + loop.run_in_executor for true CPU-bound work. Or move it out of the request path entirely (Celery worker).

title: Never use .all() on unbounded SQLAlchemy queries
content:
Trigger: results = session.query(Model).filter(...).all() without a limit, paging, or yield_per.

Consequence: loads the full table into memory. At 10K rows it's fine. At 10M rows the worker OOMs and takes the box down.

Alternative: explicit .limit(N), or stream with .yield_per(1000) for batches. For pagination, cursor-based (last_id) beats offset-based at scale.

title: Never commit .env files or credentials.json
content:
Trigger: fresh project, .env added to track config alongside the code.

Consequence: secrets in git history. Public repo: scanners notify within minutes; private repo: any contributor with read access has every secret you've ever used.

Alternative: .env in .gitignore from commit 1. Provide .env.example with placeholder values. CI uses environment variables or a secret manager.

title: Never trust user input in subprocess shell=True
content:
Trigger: subprocess.run(f'git clone {user_url}', shell=True).

Consequence: command injection. user_url='; rm -rf $HOME #' executes the second command on most shells.

Alternative: shell=False with a list of args: subprocess.run(['git', 'clone', user_url]). Still validate the URL before passing — git itself trusts the URL but a malformed value can cause side effects.

title: Never log sensitive data verbatim
content:
Trigger: logger.info(f'request body: {body}') without thinking about what's in body.

Consequence: passwords, tokens, PII, payment data show up in CloudWatch / Datadog / wherever. Compliance violation, GDPR exposure, retroactive nightmare.

Alternative: structured logging with explicit field allowlist. Mask known-sensitive keys (password, token, ssn, card_number). pydantic-settings has SecretStr that prints as '***' in repr.

title: Never use SQL string concatenation with user input
content:
Trigger: f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = \'{name}\'' or '%s' % style without parameterization.

Consequence: SQL injection. Classic OWASP top 10. Read-only sites get their schema dumped; read-write sites get owned.

Alternative: parameterized queries always. SQLAlchemy: session.query(...).filter(User.name == name) or text(':name') with params. Raw psycopg: cursor.execute('SELECT ... WHERE name = %s', (name,)) — never % formatting.

title: PostgreSQL over SQLite for production web services
content:
Chose PostgreSQL. Alternatives considered: SQLite, MySQL, MongoDB.

Reason: SQLite serializes all writes through a single writer; concurrent web request load thrashes the WAL. MySQL is fine but the Postgres ecosystem (JSONB, full-text, listen/notify, partial indexes, mature ORMs) is stronger. MongoDB is wrong shape for relational data with strong consistency needs.

Reversible: yes, but expensive once dataset grows. Alembic + SQLAlchemy abstract most away; stored procs, JSONB queries, and listen/notify lock you in.

title: FastAPI over Flask for new async services
content:
Chose FastAPI. Alternatives considered: Flask + flask-restx, Django REST framework, Starlette directly.

Reason: native async; Pydantic-based request/response validation; auto-generated OpenAPI; type checking from request to response. Flask requires multiple plugins glued together. DRF is fine for Django-shaped projects but heavy for service APIs.

Reversible: yes; routes can be ported, but Pydantic models permeate the codebase and unwinding takes weeks.

title: uv over pip + pip-tools for dependency management
content:
Chose uv. Alternatives considered: pip + pip-tools, Poetry, PDM, Hatch.

Reason: 10-100x faster than pip, native lockfile (uv.lock), works with existing pyproject.toml, no Python toolchain dependency (single Rust binary). Poetry is mature but slow and has its own pyproject.toml dialect. PDM and Hatch are fine but smaller community.

Reversible: yes; uv pip install is a pip drop-in, removing uv.lock and going back to pip-tools is a one-day migration.

title: Pre-commit hooks reduce CI failures by ~60%
content:
Wire ruff check, ruff format, mypy strict, and pytest --collect-only as pre-commit hooks. Most syntax + style + import-order issues never reach CI; the team learns to fix locally before push. CI then catches only logic + integration failures.

Why: short feedback loops change behaviour. Discovering a lint failure 30 seconds after commit teaches faster than discovering it 5 minutes into a CI run.

When: every Python project, day one. The pre-commit framework handles config + multi-language hooks well.

title: Structured logging with correlation IDs speeds debugging 5x
content:
Use structlog (or stdlib logging with a JSON formatter). Inject a correlation_id at request entry — middleware grabs/generates X-Request-ID and binds it via contextvars. Every downstream log line carries it.

Why: in production, the only way to trace 'what happened during this request' is to grep all logs for one ID. Without correlation, you're matching timestamps across services and getting it wrong.

When: any service that runs in production with more than one user.

title: Exponential backoff with jitter for retries
content:
On retry: sleep base * 2^attempt + random.uniform(0, base) seconds. Cap at a max (30s). Retry only idempotent operations (GET, PUT, DELETE; POST only if you have an idempotency key).

Why: thundering herd — many clients retrying at the same fixed interval pile up on the recovering server. Jitter spreads them.

When: any external API call. Use the tenacity library (@retry decorator) instead of hand-rolling.

title: Pre-build Docker images with --target=runtime stage
content:
Multi-stage Dockerfile: builder stage installs deps + builds wheel; runtime stage copies the wheel and minimal artifacts. FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime is ~150 MB vs full python:3.12 at ~1 GB.

Why: smaller image = faster pulls, smaller attack surface, faster Kubernetes scheduling.

When: any production Dockerfile.

title: Healthcheck endpoint returns version + git SHA
content:
GET /healthz returns {"status": "ok", "version": "1.2.3", "sha": "abc1234", "db": "connected"}. Build pipeline injects sha as env var; Pydantic settings reads it.

Why: in incident review, knowing exactly which build is responding to /healthz at time T is half the diagnosis. Without it, you're matching deploy times to log times by hand.

When: every service. Kubernetes liveness uses the same endpoint; the version field is for humans.

asyncio.gather(*tasks) without return_exceptions=True cancels remaining tasks on the first exception and re-raises. With return_exceptions=True, every task runs to completion and exceptions appear in the result list as values.

Why: 'fan-out then fail' is the wrong default for batch work. One slow upstream returning 500 shouldn't lose the other nine successes. Inspect the result list, log the exceptions, return the partial successes.

When: independent parallel work where partial success is acceptable. For all-or-nothing semantics, use the default (or asyncio.TaskGroup which is clearer).
title: asyncio.gather(return_exceptions=True) for partial failures

content:
Python 3.11+ asyncio.TaskGroup is a context manager that owns its tasks. On exit, all tasks complete or all are cancelled together. Exceptions are wrapped in ExceptionGroup so multiple failures surface, not just the first.

Why: gather() leaks tasks on cancellation in subtle cases (the cancel happens but the coroutine isn't awaited). TaskGroup makes the lifetime explicit and matches anyio/trio's nursery model.

When: any new code on Python 3.11+ that fans out work and joins it. gather() is fine for one-shot batches without cancellation concerns.
title: asyncio.TaskGroup over asyncio.gather for structured concurrency

content:
Python 3.11+ async with asyncio.timeout(5): is the recommended timeout primitive. wait_for wraps a single coroutine; timeout() bounds an arbitrary block including TaskGroups and nested awaits.

Why: wait_for has subtle cancellation-vs-timeout races (the inner task may complete just as the timeout fires, dropping the result). timeout() composes with structured concurrency cleanly.

When: any async block that needs an upper bound. Use asyncio.timeout_at() when you have an absolute deadline to share across multiple awaits.
title: asyncio.timeout context over asyncio.wait_for

content:
Bind correlation_id, tenant_id, user_id into a contextvars.ContextVar at request entry. Loggers and downstream calls read the var without threading the value through every signature.

Why: passing context as kwargs pollutes every layer with framework concerns. ContextVar is asyncio-aware — each Task gets its own copy on creation, no cross-talk between concurrent requests.

When: any per-request datum the whole stack needs (correlation id, locale, feature flags). For data the call actually computes on, use arguments.
title: Propagate request context with contextvars, not function args

content:
Trigger: try/except Exception inside an async function that catches CancelledError and logs without re-raising.

Consequence: the task ignores cancellation. Graceful shutdown hangs; tests with asyncio.timeout never return; structured concurrency primitives leak tasks because the coroutine refuses to die.

Alternative: catch CancelledError explicitly and re-raise after cleanup. In Python 3.8+, CancelledError is a BaseException — except Exception does NOT catch it, but except: does. Be specific: except (HTTPError, OSError).
title: Never swallow asyncio.CancelledError without re-raising

content:
Register a signal handler in the lifespan that sets a shutdown event; in-flight requests complete, new connections are refused, then the loop exits. Uvicorn does this for you; bespoke asyncio servers must wire signal.signal or loop.add_signal_handler.

Why: Kubernetes sends SIGTERM and waits terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s) before SIGKILL. Without graceful handling, in-flight requests get cut mid-write, clients see resets, dashboards spike on every deploy.

When: every long-running async service. PreStop hook is a fallback, not a substitute for in-process signal handling.
title: Handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown in async services

content:
Trigger: for user in users: print(user.posts). Each access on .posts fires a fresh SELECT. 100 users = 101 queries.

Consequence: the classic N+1. Latency that's invisible on dev (10 rows) becomes a 5s response on prod (1000 rows). Slow query log fills with identical SELECTs differing only by id.

Alternative: session.scalars(select(User).options(selectinload(User.posts))) loads posts in one extra IN-list query. joinedload() for one-to-one, selectinload() for one-to-many. Add an alembic + sqlalchemy event handler that warns on lazy loads in prod (event.listens_for(Engine, 'before_cursor_execute')).
title: Never iterate a SQLAlchemy relationship without selectinload

content:
Trigger: SQLAlchemy or asyncpg connecting through pgbouncer in 'transaction' pool mode while leaving prepared statements enabled (the asyncpg default).

Consequence: 'prepared statement "__asyncpg_stmt_X__" does not exist' under load. The next transaction lands on a different physical connection that never saw the PREPARE. Errors are intermittent and only show up at concurrency.

Alternative: pass statement_cache_size=0 to asyncpg, or set prepared_statement_cache_size=0 on the SQLAlchemy URL. Use 'session' pool mode if you need prepared statements (loses some pooling benefit). Or skip pgbouncer entirely if your driver pools well — asyncpg + pool_size tuning often beats pgbouncer transaction mode for Python services.
title: Never run pgbouncer transaction pooling with prepared statements

content:
Adding a NOT NULL column to a populated table on Postgres is a full table rewrite under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock. Split into three deploys: (1) ALTER ADD COLUMN nullable; (2) backfill in batches; (3) ALTER SET NOT NULL after every row is populated.

Why: a single 'add NOT NULL with DEFAULT' on a 100M row table locks reads and writes for minutes. Three small migrations keep the lock windows short and let you abort between steps.

When: every column add on a table with meaningful row count. Tools like pg-osc, pt-online-schema-change, or pgroll automate the dance for the largest cases.
title: Online schema changes: add nullable, backfill, then enforce

content:
Trigger: 'rename users.email_address to users.email' in one alembic migration, deployed alongside the code that reads the new name.

Consequence: rolling deploy means old pods read old name, new pods read new name. Whichever the migration committed becomes a 500 on the other half. Roll back the code and the schema is now inconsistent with old code too.

Alternative: expand-contract. Deploy 1: add new column, dual-write. Deploy 2: backfill + read from new. Deploy 3: drop old column. Slow but never breaks. Same pattern for renaming tables, splitting columns, changing types.
title: Never rename a column in a single deploy

content:
CREATE INDEX ix_doc_data ON documents USING GIN (data jsonb_path_ops); — supports @> containment efficiently. The default GIN opclass also indexes existence (?) and key-value lookups but is bigger; jsonb_path_ops is ~10x smaller and faster for @> alone.

Why: querying JSONB without a GIN index is a full table scan disguised as a key lookup. PostgreSQL won't warn — the query just gets slower as data grows.

When: any JSONB column you filter on. For specific path lookups, a btree expression index on (data->>'field') is cheaper than a GIN.
title: Index JSONB with GIN for containment queries

content:
Return ?cursor=<opaque> from each page; the cursor encodes the last row's sort key. Next page does WHERE created_at < :cursor LIMIT 20. Stable under inserts, indexed lookup, no count query.

Why: OFFSET 100000 scans 100,000 rows it then discards. Latency grows linearly with page number. Inserts during pagination shift offsets and skip/duplicate rows.

When: any list endpoint that can grow past ~1000 rows. Encode the cursor as base64(json) so clients treat it as opaque and you can change the encoding later.
title: Cursor pagination over offset for large lists

content:
Client generates a UUID per logical request, sends it as Idempotency-Key. Server stores (key, response) for 24h. Replay returns the stored response without re-executing.

Why: networks drop responses, clients retry. Without idempotency keys, a charge-card POST that times out at the LB becomes a double charge. Stripe's API made this the industry default for a reason.

When: any mutating endpoint where double execution has user-visible cost (payments, sends, posts). Cheap to add up front; expensive to retrofit after the first incident.
title: Idempotency-Key header on every state-changing POST

content:
{"type": "https://errors.example.com/validation", "title": "Validation failed", "status": 422, "detail": "...", "instance": "/orders/123"}. Content-Type: application/problem+json.

Why: clients written against three different error shapes (FastAPI default, your custom, the stack trace) need three error parsers. RFC 7807 is the boring standard; pick it and stop arguing.

When: any external API. FastAPI's HTTPException + a custom exception handler that emits the problem+json shape is ~20 lines.
title: Error responses follow RFC 7807 problem+json

content:
orjson.dumps is 2-10x faster than json.dumps and handles datetime, UUID, dataclasses natively. Returns bytes, not str — feed directly to Response(content=orjson.dumps(x), media_type='application/json').

Why: at scale, JSON serialization is a measurable fraction of request CPU. FastAPI's default uses stdlib json; switching to ORJSONResponse drops p99 latency 5-15% on JSON-heavy endpoints.

When: any high-throughput JSON API. Set default_response_class=ORJSONResponse on the FastAPI app for global effect.
title: orjson over stdlib json for API serialization

content:
Trigger: open('/tmp/upload', 'wb').write(body) inside async def handler. Looks fine in dev.

Consequence: the open + write block the event loop. Other concurrent requests stall for the duration; a slow disk turns one request into a service-wide latency spike.

Alternative: aiofiles for async open/read/write, or asyncio.to_thread(open(...).write, body) for one-off operations. For uploads, stream UploadFile chunks (await file.read(chunk_size)) to bound memory while staying async.
title: Never call sync file I/O from an async handler

content:
Read: try cache, miss → fetch from DB, write cache with TTL, return. On miss for a hot key under load, multiple workers fetch simultaneously (the stampede). Use a short distributed lock (SETNX with TTL) around the fetch so only one worker recomputes.

Why: cache-aside is the default pattern but the unprotected version DDoSes your DB on every TTL expiry of a hot key. Hot keys exist whether you planned for them or not.

When: any read-heavy endpoint backed by a cache. Below ~100 RPS the stampede is invisible; above it, the lock is mandatory.
title: Cache-aside with explicit TTL and stampede protection

content:
from httpx import AsyncClient, ASGITransport; async with AsyncClient(transport=ASGITransport(app=app), base_url='http://test') as ac: r = await ac.get('/'). Drives the FastAPI app in-process, fully async, no event-loop juggling.

Why: starlette.testclient.TestClient runs sync — it spins up a thread for the async app and tests can deadlock on shared async resources (DB session, redis pool). httpx.AsyncClient is the same code path the real client uses.

When: any async FastAPI test that touches async dependencies. Sync TestClient is fine for routes with no async work behind them.
title: httpx.AsyncClient with ASGITransport over TestClient for async tests

content:
pip install pytest-randomly. It shuffles test order each run and prints the seed. A test that passes in alphabetical order but fails on shuffle is leaking state via globals, env vars, or shared DB rows.

Why: order-dependent tests pass for months then fail when an unrelated test is added. The leak is real; the order just hides it. Random order forces the leak into the open while the change that caused it is still fresh.

When: every Python project. Pin the seed in CI when reproducing a failure: pytest -p randomly --randomly-seed=12345.
title: pytest-randomly to surface order-dependent test failures
hypothesis for property-based testing of pure logic

content:
Trigger: copying the dev command (uvicorn app:app --reload) into the Dockerfile or systemd unit.

Consequence: a watchdog process polls the filesystem every second. Wastes CPU. Worse, file changes during deploy can trigger a mid-request reload, dropping in-flight connections.

Alternative: production runs uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers N (or behind gunicorn -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker for graceful worker recycling). --reload is a dev-only flag.
title: Never run uvicorn --reload in production

content:
Sync gunicorn workers (Flask, Django WSGI): each worker handles one request at a time, so 2*CPU+1 maximises throughput while leaving headroom for the OS. Async workers (UvicornWorker, Hypercorn): each worker is concurrent; CPU count is the right cap because more workers fight for the GIL on CPU-bound bits.

Why: too few workers underutilise the box; too many thrash on context switches and memory. The formula is empirical but holds up across most apps.

When: every gunicorn/uvicorn deploy. Tune by load testing — the formula is a starting point, not gospel.
title: Worker count: (2 × CPU) + 1 for sync, CPU count for async

content:
GET /healthz: returns 200 if the process is alive (no DB, no Redis). GET /ready: returns 200 only if all deps respond. Kubernetes liveness probes /healthz, readiness probes /ready.

Why: a deep liveness probe restarts the pod when the database is briefly slow — cascading the database outage into a pod-restart storm. Liveness asks 'is this process broken?', not 'can it serve traffic right now?'.

When: every Kubernetes deployment. Probe timeouts and intervals matter — keep liveness fast (<1s) to avoid false restarts.
title: Healthcheck: cheap liveness, separate deep readiness

content:
[tool.mypy] strict = true in pyproject.toml. Add # mypy: disable-error-code=name to specific files when third-party stubs lag. Never set strict=false project-wide once it's been on.

Why: mypy strict catches Optional misuse, missing returns, untyped functions — the bug classes that account for most production NoneType errors. Adding strict mode later means hundreds of preexisting violations and a 'fix later' backlog that never gets fixed.

When: every new Python project. For migrations, enable strict per-package via [[tool.mypy.overrides]] and convert directory by directory.
title: mypy strict from day one, opt out per file

content:
UserId = NewType('UserId', str); OrderId = NewType('OrderId', str). Now def get_user(id: UserId) refuses an OrderId at type check time, even though both are str at runtime.

Why: 'pass the order id where a user id was expected' is a real bug class. NewType has zero runtime cost — it's pure type-checker hint — and catches the mismatch at the call site.

When: any domain with multiple opaque-string identifiers. The pattern scales: AccountId, SessionId, JobId.
title: NewType for IDs to prevent cross-type assignments

content:
class DBSettings(BaseModel): host: str; port: int. class Settings(BaseSettings): db: DBSettings; model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_nested_delimiter='__'). Then DB__HOST=localhost DB__PORT=5432 populates nested config.

Why: flat env vars (DB_HOST, DB_PORT, DB_USER) become unmanageable at scale. Nested config groups related settings and matches the structure your code already reads.

When: any service with more than ~5 config values per subsystem. Two-underscore delimiter is the convention; pick it and document it.
title: pydantic-settings nested config via env_nested_delimiter

content:
Trigger: CORSMiddleware(app, allow_origins=['*'], allow_credentials=True).

Consequence: browsers ignore the wildcard when credentials are sent — the request fails with no clear error. Worse, if you 'fix' it by reflecting Origin back as Access-Control-Allow-Origin, any site can read authenticated responses on behalf of the user.

Alternative: explicit allow_origins=['https://app.example.com', 'https://admin.example.com']. Or skip credentials entirely and use bearer tokens. Wildcard origin is fine for public APIs without cookies.
title: Never set CORS allow_origins='*' with allow_credentials=True

content:
Chose argon2id. Alternatives considered: bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2.

Reason: argon2id is the OWASP-recommended default since 2021. Memory-hard so GPU attacks scale poorly; configurable time/memory parameters; tuneable per hardware. bcrypt is fine but capped at 72-byte input and lacks memory hardness. PBKDF2 is approved for FIPS but weakest against GPUs.

Reversible: yes, but expensive at scale. Hash on next successful login: if the stored hash is bcrypt and the password verifies, re-hash with argon2id and update. Both hashes can coexist during migration.
title: argon2id over bcrypt for new password hashes

content:
Refresh endpoint accepts the old refresh token, issues a new access + new refresh, invalidates the old refresh in a server-side store. Reuse of an old refresh token = breach signal: revoke the entire family.

Why: a stolen refresh token is a long-lived credential. Single-use rotation means the legitimate user's next refresh invalidates the attacker's token (or vice versa) and the mismatch is detectable.

When: any auth system using refresh tokens. Static long-lived refresh tokens are an anti-pattern; they exist only because rotation is more code.
title: Rotate JWT refresh tokens on every use
model_validator(mode='after') for cross-field rules

content:
pip install opentelemetry-instrumentation-fastapi opentelemetry-instrumentation-sqlalchemy. Call FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app) in lifespan; spans appear automatically for every request and query.

Why: writing trace spans by hand drifts. Auto-instrumentation gives 80% coverage in 10 lines, and the trace IDs propagate to logs (when you add the LoggingInstrumentor too).

When: any service that lives next to other services. Single-service apps may not need it; the moment you add a second service, traces are how you debug both.
title: OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation for HTTP + DB tracing
